perturbation.original_text: 5. This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted, and its performance shall be governed by substantive laws of the state of Connecticut, U.S.A., without recourse to its conflict of laws, rules or principles.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted, and its performance shall be governed by the laws of the jurisdiction chosen by UCAR CARBON, without recourse to its conflict of laws, rules or principles.
perturbation.explanation: By allowing UCAR CARBON to choose the jurisdiction unilaterally, the agreement could potentially violate Connecticut's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which is a part of Connecticut contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification allows UCAR CARBON to potentially select a jurisdiction with laws unfavorable to Ballard Power Systems, thus violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing inherent in Connecticut contract law, as Connecticut General Statutes § 42-110b prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Connecticut General Statutes § 42-110b. (2023) - Unfair trade practices prohibited. Gen. Stat. 2023 Conn. Gen. Stat. (here) 2022 Conn. Gen. Stat. View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Connecticut General Statutes Title 42 - Business, Selling, Trading and Collection Practices Chapter 735a - Unfair Trade Practices Section 42-110b. - Unfair trade practices prohibited. Legislative intent. Universal Citation: CT Gen Stat § 42-110b. (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) No person shall engage in unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing subsection (a) of this section, the commissioner and the courts of this state shall be guided by interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the federal courts to Section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 USC 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The commissioner may, in accordance with chapter 54, establish by regulation acts, practices or methods which shall be deemed to be unfair or deceptive in violation of subsection (a) of this section. Such regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the federal trade commission and the federal courts in interpreting the provisions of the Federal Trade Commission Act. (d) It is the intention of the legislature that this chapter be remedial and be so construed. (P.A. 73-615, S. 2, 16; P.A. 75-618, S. 1, 11; P.A. 76-303, S. 1, 4.) History; P.A. 75-618 rephrased provisions for clarity and consistency; P.A. 76-303 deleted provision in Subsec. (a) which established unfa
perturbation.justification: The modification allows UCAR CARBON to unilaterally choose the jurisdiction, potentially selecting one with laws unfavorable to Ballard Power Systems. This violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which Connecticut General Statutes § 42-110b addresses by prohibiting unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. The original text specified Connecticut law, which provides certain protections. The changed text removes this and allows for potentially unfair practices by empowering one party to choose a jurisdiction that might disadvantage the other. This directly contradicts the intent of § 42-110b to prevent unfair practices.

perturbation.original_text: During the term of this Agreement, the receiving party agrees to hold in confidence and not disclose to any person or persons, other than its employees with a need to know, or use except for the purpose of this agreement, for a period of fifteen (15) years from the date of each disclosure, any and all Proprietary Information disclosed in writing and identified as Proprietary Information by the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: During the term of this Agreement, the receiving party agrees to hold in confidence and not disclose to any person or persons, other than its employees with a need to know, or use except for the purpose of this agreement, for a period of one (1) year from the date of each disclosure, any and all Proprietary Information disclosed in writing and identified as Proprietary Information by the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period from 15 years to 1 year could be deemed an unreasonably short duration for trade secret protection under Connecticut law, potentially voiding the protection of valuable trade secrets. Connecticut adopts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Connecticut General Statutes § 35-51 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification reduces the protection period for proprietary information to one year. This may not be sufficient time to protect trade secrets, especially in fields like fuel cell technology where development cycles can be lengthy. Connecticut's Uniform Trade Secrets Act aims to protect trade secrets for a reasonable duration, and a one-year period may not meet this standard depending on the nature of the secret, thus creating potential legal issues if a trade secret is misappropriated after that year.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from 15 years to 1 year, potentially failing to protect trade secrets for a reasonable duration, as the law explanation states that Connecticut's Uniform Trade Secrets Act aims to protect trade secrets for a reasonable duration. The snippet provided does not directly state this but the law explanation clearly alludes to a Connecticut law based on the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. A one-year period may not meet this standard depending on the nature of the secret. This constitutes an omission, specifically, an Omission Legal Contradiction, as it removes essential information regarding the adequate timeframe needed to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party agrees to use at least the same degree of care in maintaining the other party's Proprietary Information confidential as it does for maintaining the confidentiality of its own Proprietary Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party agrees to use reasonable efforts in maintaining the other party's Proprietary Information confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'at least the same degree of care' with 'reasonable efforts' weakens the standard of care. This could be problematic under Connecticut trade secret law, which expects a higher level of protection for confidential information and trade secrets. Trade secret misappropriation can occur when reasonable measures to protect confidentiality are not taken.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Connecticut General Statutes § 35-51 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the standard of care to 'reasonable efforts', the contract may fall short of the measures required to maintain trade secret status under the Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act, potentially leaving the disclosing party without legal recourse if the information is misappropriated. The original text provided a higher standard of care, relative to their own confidential information.

Law and Legal Definition Et seq. is an abbreviation of the Latin phrase "et sequentes" (masculine and feminine plural) or "et sequentia" (neuter), which mean "and the following one or ones." It is used to refer to a page citation that continues on the pages that follow, or a statutory section or subsection and the sections or subsections that follow it. perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates 'at least the same degree of care,' setting a high standard for protecting proprietary information. The changed text weakens this standard to 'reasonable efforts.' The Law Explanation clarifies that this change may not meet the requirements of the Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act, potentially jeopardizing trade secret status. While neither snippet directly addresses trade secret law or 'reasonable efforts,' the Perturbation and Law Explanations both suggest a lower standard of care is insufficient, thus creating a legal contradiction by potentially failing to adequately protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: If Proprietary Information is disclosed orally or in other than written form, it must be identified as Proprietary Information at the time of disclosure and summarized in writing and identified as Proprietary Information by the disclosing party within thirty (30) days from the date of the disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: If Proprietary Information is disclosed orally or in other than written form, it may be identified as Proprietary Information at the time of disclosure and may be summarized in writing and identified as Proprietary Information by the disclosing party at their convenience.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'must' to 'may' and replacing 'within thirty (30) days' with 'at their convenience' weakens the requirement to protect orally disclosed information. This introduces ambiguity and makes it harder to enforce confidentiality for such disclosures under Connecticut law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Connecticut General Statutes § 35-51 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of information to qualify for trade secret protection. By making the identification and summarization of orally disclosed information optional and delayed, the contract fails to ensure these reasonable efforts are taken. The change creates a legal contradiction to the requirements for trade secret protection.

All states Alabama Alaska Arizona Arkansas California Colorado Connecticut Delaware District of Columbia Florida Georgia Hawaii Idaho Illinois Indiana Iowa Kansas Kentucky Louisiana Maine Maryland Massachusetts Michigan Minnesota Mississippi Missouri Montana Nebraska Nevada New Hampshire New Jersey New Mexico New York North Carolina North Dakota Ohio Oklahoma Oregon Pennsylvania Rhode Island South Carolina South Dakota Tennessee Texas Utah Vermont Virginia Washington West Virginia Wisconsin Wyoming Select your State Alabama Alaska Arizona Arkansas California Colorado Connecticut Delaware District of Columbia Florida Georgia Hawaii Idaho Illinois Indiana Iowa Kansas Kentucky Louisiana Maine Maryland Massachusetts Michigan Minnesota Mississippi Missouri Montana Nebraska Nevada New Hampshire New Jersey New Mexico New York North Carolina North Dakota Ohio Oklahoma Oregon Pennsylvania Rhode Island South Carolina South Dakota Tennessee Texas Utah Vermont Virginia Washington West Virginia Wisconsin Wyoming Search Popular forms Show Last Will and Testament Power of Attorney Promissory Note LLC Operating Agreement Living Will Rental Lease Agreement Non-Disclosure Agreement Statute Law and Legal Definition Statute is the law passed by a legislative body. It is the written law passed by legislature, parliament or elected or appointed houses of assembly on the state or federal level. A statute may forbid a certain act, direct a certain act, make a declaration, or set forth governmental mechanisms to aid society. A statute begins as a bill proposed or sponsored by a legislator. If the bill survives the legislative committee process and is approved by both houses of the legislature, the bill becomes law when it is signed by the executive officer (the president on the federal level or the governor on the state level). When a bill becomes law, the various provisions in the bill are called statutes. The term statute signifies the elevation of a bill from legislative proposal to law. A statute is also called legislation. perturbation.justification: The change from 'must' to 'may' and replacing a specific timeframe ('within thirty (30) days') with 'at their convenience' significantly weakens the obligation to protect proprietary information disclosed orally. The Law Explanation says that the Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of information. By making the identification and summarization of orally disclosed information optional and delayed, the modified contract fails to ensure reasonable efforts are taken. Scraped law snippets do not directly apply.
